item.title: Venice, from the Porch of Madonna della Salute
item.museum: The Metropolitan Museum of Art, New York
item.lesson: The Englishman arrives, and the stone begins to dissolve. Turner stands roughly where Guardi stood — on the steps of the Salute, the church from yesterday's painting — but he faces the other way, up the Grand Canal, and what he sees is barely architecture at all. The palaces on the right advance like a fleet of clouds; the sky and the water have begun trading places. Turner visited Venice three times, fewer than forty days in total, and painted it for the rest of his life, because Venice had shown him what he already suspected: that a city built on water is really built on light, and light does not hold still for measurement. When this canvas hung at the Royal Academy in 1835, critics complained his buildings would not stand up. They were answering the wrong question. Turner was not asking whether Venice could be documented. He was asking how much of it could burn away and still be Venice.
item.lookFor: Notice how the gondolas in the foreground are more solid than the city behind them — Turner keeps one plank of the real world underfoot, then lets everything beyond it evaporate.
